label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's look at the passage carefully. The passage mentions, "One morning last week, for example, I got up and found that I had left the front door unlocked all night, yet I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." So the writer found the door unlocked, but he clearly remembers locking it the night before. This means that he believes he locked the door, but in reality, the door was left unlocked. Therefore, he forgot that he didn't lock the door (he thought he locked it, but actually didn't). Looking at the options: (A) One night the writer forgot to lock the front door. - This implies that he didn't lock it and he forgot that he didn't lock it. But according to the passage, he remembers locking it (he clearly remembers locking it), so this is not correct. (B) One night the writer forgot having locked the front door. - This suggests that he did lock the door but forgot that he did. However, the passage says he clearly remembers locking it, so this is not correct either. (C) The writer remembered to lock the door. - This suggests that he locked the door and remembered to do so. But according to the passage, the door was actually left unlocked, so this is not correct. (D) the writer remembered unlocking the front door. - This suggests that he remembered unlocking it, which is not what the passage says. The passage says he remembered locking it. None of the options seem to perfectly match the situation described. But let's re-read the passage: "I had left the front door unlocked all night, yet I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." So the writer actually left the door unlocked (he forgot to lock it), but he remembers having locked it (he has a false memory of locking it). This is a case of memory "trick" where he remembers something that didn't actually happen. This is a bit tricky, but let's analyze the options again: (A) One night the writer forgot to lock the front door. - This is true in the sense that he didn't lock it (he left it unlocked), but the passage doesn't say he forgot that he didn't lock it. It says he "clearly remember locking it carefully", so he didn't forget that he didn't lock it, he had a false memory of locking it. (B) One night the writer forgot having locked the front door. - This is not correct because he didn't forget
